### 1D Features
The primary sequence of Protein 2 (624 aa) from Oscillospiraceae suggests its role as a secreted carbohydrate-degrading enzyme. Unlike integral membrane proteins, its architecture points towards a highly structured catalytic core flanked by flexible regions, essential for operating in the extracellular or periplasmic space to metabolize complex plant polysaccharides from the host's diet.

#### **Transmembrane topology (DeepTMHMM)**
In order to predict the transmembrane topology of Protein 2, the server of [DeepTMHMM](https://dtu.biolib.com/DeepTMHMM) was used as it is a well-known deep-learning based tool designed to identify transmembrane helices and signal peptides from the amino acid sequence.

The analysis revealed a complete absence of Transmembrane Regions (TMRs). Instead, a well-defined N-terminal signal peptide was identified spanning residues 1 to 55. The remaining sequence (residues 56-624) is predicted to be located on the outside (extracellular space). This topology confirms that the target is not a membrane-resident protein, but rather a secreted molecule whose signal peptide is cleaved prior to its functional maturation (@fig-DeepTMHMM-Protein2).

![DeepTMHMM Most Likely Topology and Posterior probabilities for Protein 2](/pics/Protein2/plot_prediction_protein2.png){#fig-DeepTMHMM-Protein2}

#### **Intrinsic disorder prediction (IUPred3)**
To predict the propensity of each amino acid to be located in intrinsically disordered regions, [IUPred3](https://iupred3.elte.hu/) was used. The results are shown in @fig-IUPred3-Protein2.

The prediction highlighted two main regions of intrinsic disorder. The most prominent peak (score > 0.8) occurs between residues 60 and 85, immediately following the signal peptide cleavage site, likely acting as a highly flexible linker. A secondary disordered loop was predicted around residues 470-500. Identifying these flexible regions is crucial, as they are expected to display low confidence scores (e.g., low pLDDT) in subsequent Deep Learning 3D models, reflecting their natural structural flexibility rather than an algorithm failure.

![IUPred3 Intrinsic disorder regions prediction for Protein 2](/pics/Protein2/plot_disorded_protein2_iupred3.png){#fig-IUPred3-Protein2}

#### **Functional annotation and domain architecture (InterProScan)**
To infer the biological role and structural domains of this uncharacterized protein, [InterProScan](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/interpro/about/interproscan/) was utilized as an additional 1D sequence analysis tool.

The analysis yielded highly informative matches. A highly stable, structured central domain (residues 152-392) was confidently annotated as belonging to the Pectate Lyase family (Pectate_lyase_3 / Pectin lyase-like) @fig-InterPro-Protein2. Interestingly, structural domain matches corresponding to Phage tail proteins (Phage_tail_b) were also reported. Both families frequently share a similar structural architecture, specifically the parallel beta-helix fold, which is highly efficient at binding and cleaving complex carbohydrates.

he identification of a Pectate Lyase (Family 3) catalytic core is highly significant, as enzymes in this family are typically characterized as calcium-dependent metalloenzymes. In these proteins, $Ca^{2+}$ ions are essential both for stabilizing the parallel beta-helix architecture and for coordinating the substrate during catalysis. Therefore, the presence of calcium is considered a critical requirement for the structural and functional integrity of Protein 2, a hypothesis that will be further explored through 3D multimeric modeling.

![Results of InterProScan for Protein 2](/pics/Protein2/results_interpro_protein2.png){#fig-InterPro-Protein2}

#### **Combined 1D analysis**
In conclusion, the combined 1D analysis reveals that Protein 2 is a secreted enzyme, lacking transmembrane segments and relying on an N-terminal signal peptide for its translocation. Its architecture consists of a highly structured central beta-helix core—characteristic of pectate lyases and phage tail proteins—flanked and interrupted by highly flexible intrinsically disordered regions. Overall, this organization supports a cohesive biological hypothesis: a viral carbohydrate-degrading structural gene was potentially co-opted by the gut bacterium to metabolize complex dietary polysaccharides.
